instruction: How do I use Zod for runtime validation in TypeScript? I want to validate API responses and get proper TypeScript types from the schema.
output:
<think>
Zod solves the fundamental TypeScript gap: TS types disappear at runtime, so you can't validate that an API response actually matches your type. Zod lets you define a schema once and derive both runtime validation AND TypeScript types from it.

The key insight is `z.infer<typeof schema>` — this extracts the TS type from the Zod schema, so you never define the type twice. The schema IS the type definition, with runtime checking as a bonus.

For API responses, you want `.safeParse()` not `.parse()` — safeParse returns a discriminated union `{ success: true, data } | { success: false, error }` instead of throwing. This is safer in production because you handle bad data explicitly rather than catching exceptions.

Zod also supports transforms (parse a string into a Date), defaults, optional fields, and refinements (custom validation like "email must end with @company.com"). These compose cleanly with `.pipe()` for multi-step validation.
</think>

```typescript
import { z } from 'zod';

// Define schema — this IS your type definition
const UserSchema = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
  name: z.string().min(1).max(100),
  email: z.string().email(),
  age: z.number().int().min(0).max(150),
  role: z.enum(['admin', 'user', 'moderator']),
  createdAt: z.string().datetime().transform(s => new Date(s)),
  tags: z.array(z.string()).default([]),
  metadata: z.record(z.string(), z.unknown()).optional(),
});

// Derive TypeScript type from schema — single source of truth
type User = z.infer<typeof UserSchema>;
// User = { id: string; name: string; email: string; age: number;
//          role: 'admin'|'user'|'moderator'; createdAt: Date; ... }

// Safe API response validation
async function fetchUser(id: string): Promise<User | null> {
  const response = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`);
  const json = await response.json();

  // safeParse returns discriminated union — never throws
  const result = UserSchema.safeParse(json);
  if (!result.success) {
    console.error('Invalid API response:', result.error.flatten());
    return null;
  }
  return result.data; // fully typed User, createdAt is Date
}

// Nested schemas compose
const PaginatedResponseSchema = <T extends z.ZodType>(itemSchema: T) =>
  z.object({
    items: z.array(itemSchema),
    total: z.number().int().nonneg(),
    page: z.number().int().positive(),
    pageSize: z.number().int().positive(),
    hasNext: z.boolean(),
  });

const UserListSchema = PaginatedResponseSchema(UserSchema);
type UserList = z.infer<typeof UserListSchema>;

// Refinements for custom validation
const CreateUserSchema = UserSchema.omit({ id: true, createdAt: true }).extend({
  password: z.string()
    .min(8)
    .regex(/[A-Z]/, 'Must contain uppercase')
    .regex(/[0-9]/, 'Must contain number'),
  confirmPassword: z.string(),
}).refine(
  data => data.password === data.confirmPassword,
  { message: 'Passwords must match', path: ['confirmPassword'] }
);

type CreateUser = z.infer<typeof CreateUserSchema>;
```

instruction: What's the difference between Zod and io-ts for runtime type validation in TypeScript? When would I pick one over the other?
output:
<think>
io-ts is the older, FP-oriented library built on fp-ts. It uses codecs (encoder + decoder pairs) and returns Either<Errors, A> instead of throwing. It's mathematically rigorous and composes well in fp-ts pipelines.

Zod is the newer, more ergonomic library. It has a chainable API, better error messages, and doesn't require understanding fp-ts concepts. It's the de facto standard for most TypeScript projects now.

Pick io-ts when: your codebase already uses fp-ts extensively, you need bidirectional codecs (encode AND decode), or you're doing heavy FP composition with pipe/flow.

Pick Zod when: you want the simplest API, best documentation, largest ecosystem, and don't use fp-ts. Zod also has better integration with form libraries (react-hook-form), tRPC, and Next.js.

Performance is similar for both. The main practical difference is ergonomics and ecosystem. Zod wins for 90% of projects.
</think>

```typescript
// ========= io-ts approach =========
import * as t from 'io-ts';
import { pipe } from 'fp-ts/function';
import * as E from 'fp-ts/Either';
import { PathReporter } from 'io-ts/PathReporter';

// io-ts: codec defines both decode AND encode
const IoUser = t.type({
  id: t.string,
  name: t.string,
  age: t.number,
  active: t.boolean,
});

type IoUserType = t.TypeOf<typeof IoUser>;

// Decoding returns Either<Errors, T> — fp-ts style
function parseWithIoTs(data: unknown): IoUserType | null {
  const result = IoUser.decode(data);
  return pipe(
    result,
    E.fold(
      errors => {
        console.error(PathReporter.report(E.left(errors)));
        return null;
      },
      user => user
    )
  );
}

// ========= Zod approach =========
import { z } from 'zod';

// Zod: chainable, readable, great errors
const ZodUser = z.object({
  id: z.string(),
  name: z.string(),
  age: z.number().int().positive(),
  active: z.boolean(),
});

type ZodUserType = z.infer<typeof ZodUser>;

function parseWithZod(data: unknown): ZodUserType | null {
  const result = ZodUser.safeParse(data);
  if (!result.success) {
    console.error(result.error.flatten());
    return null;
  }
  return result.data;
}

// ========= Decision matrix =========
/*
| Feature              | Zod          | io-ts          |
|---------------------|--------------|----------------|
| Learning curve      | Low          | High (fp-ts)   |
| Error messages      | Excellent    | Verbose        |
| Ecosystem           | Huge (tRPC)  | fp-ts only     |
| Bidirectional codec | No (decode)  | Yes (enc+dec)  |
| Transforms          | Built-in     | Separate codec |
| Tree-shaking        | Good         | Excellent      |
| TypeScript inference | Excellent   | Excellent      |

Verdict: Use Zod unless you're deep in fp-ts.
*/
```

instruction: How do I write proper type guards in TypeScript that narrow types correctly and handle edge cases like null, undefined, and discriminated unions?
output:
<think>
Type guards are functions that return `x is T` — they narrow the type in the truthy branch. The compiler trusts you, so a wrong type guard is worse than no type guard (it gives false type safety).

Key patterns: (1) `typeof` for primitives, (2) `in` operator for property checking, (3) discriminated unions with a literal tag field (the best pattern), (4) custom `is` functions for complex checks.

The edge cases that bite people: `typeof null === 'object'` (must check null separately), arrays are objects (use Array.isArray), NaN is a number (must check explicitly), and empty string/0 are falsy but valid values.

For discriminated unions, the tag field must be a literal type (string literal, number literal, or boolean). This is the most reliable narrowing pattern because the compiler handles it automatically without custom guards.

A common mistake: writing a type guard that checks one property but claims to narrow to a complex type. If the guard doesn't actually verify all required properties, you have a lie in your type system.
</think>

```typescript
// === Basic type guards ===
function isString(value: unknown): value is string {
  return typeof value === 'string';
}

// typeof null === 'object' — the classic trap
function isNonNullObject(value: unknown): value is Record<string, unknown> {
  return typeof value === 'object' && value !== null && !Array.isArray(value);
}

// === Discriminated unions (best pattern) ===
type ApiResponse =
  | { status: 'success'; data: unknown; timestamp: number }
  | { status: 'error'; code: number; message: string }
  | { status: 'loading' };

function handleResponse(res: ApiResponse) {
  switch (res.status) {
    case 'success':
      // TS knows: { status: 'success'; data: unknown; timestamp: number }
      console.log(res.data, res.timestamp);
      break;
    case 'error':
      // TS knows: { status: 'error'; code: number; message: string }
      console.error(`Error ${res.code}: ${res.message}`);
      break;
    case 'loading':
      // TS knows: { status: 'loading' }
      console.log('Loading...');
      break;
    default:
      // Exhaustiveness check — errors if you miss a case
      const _exhaustive: never = res;
  }
}

// === Custom type guard with thorough validation ===
interface User {
  id: string;
  name: string;
  email: string;
  age: number;
}

// GOOD: actually validates all fields
function isUser(value: unknown): value is User {
  if (!isNonNullObject(value)) return false;
  const obj = value as Record<string, unknown>;
  return (
    typeof obj.id === 'string' &&
    typeof obj.name === 'string' &&
    typeof obj.email === 'string' &&
    typeof obj.age === 'number' &&
    !Number.isNaN(obj.age) &&  // NaN is typeof 'number'!
    obj.age >= 0
  );
}

// BAD: lies to the compiler — only checks one field
function isBadUser(value: unknown): value is User {
  return (value as any)?.id != null; // DANGEROUS: doesn't verify other fields
}

// === Assertion functions (TS 3.7+) ===
function assertDefined<T>(value: T | null | undefined, msg?: string): asserts value is T {
  if (value === null || value === undefined) {
    throw new Error(msg ?? 'Expected value to be defined');
  }
}

function processUser(maybeUser: User | null) {
  assertDefined(maybeUser, 'User required');
  // After assertion, TS knows maybeUser is User (not null)
  console.log(maybeUser.name); // no error
}

// === Array filtering with type guards ===
const mixed: (string | number | null)[] = ['hello', 42, null, 'world', null, 7];

// filter with type guard narrows the array type
const strings: string[] = mixed.filter(isString);
const nonNull: (string | number)[] = mixed.filter(
  (x): x is string | number => x !== null
);
```

instruction: I'm building a TypeScript API and want to validate request bodies, query params, and path params with Zod. How do I create a reusable middleware pattern for Express?
output:
<think>
The pattern is a generic middleware factory that takes Zod schemas for body, query, and params, validates them, and attaches typed results to the request. If validation fails, return a 400 with structured error details.

Key design decisions: (1) Make each schema optional — not every route has a body AND query AND params. (2) Use generics so the handler function receives properly typed `req.body`, `req.query`, `req.params`. (3) Return Zod's flattened errors for clear client-side error messages. (4) Put the middleware factory in a reusable function, not inline in routes.

The tricky part in Express: `req.body` is `any`, `req.query` is `ParsedQs`, `req.params` is `ParamsDictionary`. We need to override these types after validation. The cleanest approach is a wrapper that creates a validated handler with proper types.

For production, also consider: stripping unknown fields (Zod's `.strict()` or `.strip()`), coercing query string numbers (everything in query is a string), and logging validation failures for monitoring.
</think>

```typescript
import { z, ZodType, ZodError } from 'zod';
import { Request, Response, NextFunction, RequestHandler } from 'express';

// Generic validation schemas for a route
interface RouteSchemas<
  TBody extends ZodType = ZodType,
  TQuery extends ZodType = ZodType,
  TParams extends ZodType = ZodType,
> {
  body?: TBody;
  query?: TQuery;
  params?: TParams;
}

// Validated request type — overrides Express defaults with Zod output types
interface ValidatedRequest<
  TBody = unknown,
  TQuery = unknown,
  TParams = unknown,
> extends Request {
  body: TBody;
  query: TQuery & Request['query'];
  params: TParams & Request['params'];
}

// Middleware factory — validates and narrows types
function validate<
  TBody extends ZodType,
  TQuery extends ZodType,
  TParams extends ZodType,
>(schemas: RouteSchemas<TBody, TQuery, TParams>): RequestHandler {
  return (req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => {
    const errors: Record<string, z.ZodError> = {};

    if (schemas.body) {
      const result = schemas.body.safeParse(req.body);
      if (!result.success) errors.body = result.error;
      else req.body = result.data;
    }

    if (schemas.query) {
      const result = schemas.query.safeParse(req.query);
      if (!result.success) errors.query = result.error;
      else (req as any).query = result.data;
    }

    if (schemas.params) {
      const result = schemas.params.safeParse(req.params);
      if (!result.success) errors.params = result.error;
      else (req as any).params = result.data;
    }

    if (Object.keys(errors).length > 0) {
      res.status(400).json({
        error: 'Validation failed',
        details: Object.fromEntries(
          Object.entries(errors).map(([key, err]) => [key, err.flatten()])
        ),
      });
      return;
    }

    next();
  };
}

// === Usage ===
import express from 'express';

const app = express();
app.use(express.json());

// Route schemas
const CreateUserBody = z.object({
  name: z.string().min(1),
  email: z.string().email(),
  age: z.number().int().positive(),
});

const UserParams = z.object({
  id: z.string().uuid(),
});

const ListUsersQuery = z.object({
  page: z.coerce.number().int().positive().default(1),  // coerce string→number
  limit: z.coerce.number().int().min(1).max(100).default(20),
  role: z.enum(['admin', 'user']).optional(),
});

// Routes with validation
app.post('/users',
  validate({ body: CreateUserBody }),
  (req: ValidatedRequest<z.infer<typeof CreateUserBody>>, res) => {
    // req.body is fully typed: { name: string; email: string; age: number }
    res.json({ created: req.body.name });
  }
);

app.get('/users/:id',
  validate({ params: UserParams }),
  (req: ValidatedRequest<unknown, unknown, z.infer<typeof UserParams>>, res) => {
    // req.params.id is string (UUID validated)
    res.json({ id: req.params.id });
  }
);

app.get('/users',
  validate({ query: ListUsersQuery }),
  (req: ValidatedRequest<unknown, z.infer<typeof ListUsersQuery>>, res) => {
    // req.query.page is number (coerced from string), req.query.role is optional
    res.json({ page: req.query.page, limit: req.query.limit });
  }
);
```
